data.title: Shift4: Panic Sell Or Opportunistic Buy?
data.description:
Introduction

From my initial public coverage of Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR) in May 2024, Shift4 stock rallied from the $60s to the $120s by early 2025 on the back of continued robust business performance (supported by organic & inorganic expansion):

However, Shift4's announcement of a $2.5B acquisition of specialty payments and technology provider "Global Blue" in February 2025 triggered a massive sell-off in FOUR stock, which was then exacerbated by the "Liberation Day" broad market sell-off during April. While FOUR stock rebounded strongly along with the stock market in subsequent weeks over the summer, Shift4's Q2 2025 financial results and details of its $3.3B capital raise ($1.3B Senior Notes + $1B Convertible Preferred Stock (10M shares mandatory dilution) + $1B Floating Rate Term Loan) irked investors, leaving FOUR stock reeling in the $70s once again ahead of last week's quarterly report—wherein the fintech company missed headline estimates.

Now, in the immediate aftermath of Shift4's Q3 2025 report, FOUR temporarily dropped to a new 52-week low of ~$61 per share but has since rebounded back to the mid-$70s. In today's note, we shall review Shift4's Q3 2025 earnings report and reevaluate its long-term and near-term risk/reward to reach an informed investment decision. Let's go!

Shift4: By The Numbers (Q3 2025)

For Q3 2025, Shift4 marginally missed headline top- and bottom-line consensus estimates amid macro-induced consumer spending jitters, with revenue coming in at $1.18B (+29.4% y/y, miss by ~$4M) and non-GAAP EPS landing at $1.47 per share (miss by $0.01).

That said, Shift4's underlying business trends remained positive, with the company delivering healthy growth across end-to-end Payment Volume ($54.7B, up 26% y/y), GRLNF ($589.2M, +61% y/y), and adj. EBITDA ($292.1M, +56% y/y).

Now, while the step up in Shift4's financial performance in Q3 was primarily a function of inorganic growth brought on by the successful close of the Global Blue acquisition on 3rd July 2025, Shift4's organic GRLNF growth was also robust at +19% y/y.

Looking forward, Shift4's management narrowed FY-2025 guidance ranges -

  • Volume: $207-210B (+26-27% y/y) [from $200-220B]
  • GRLNF: $1.98-2.02B (+46-49% y/y) [from $1.965-2.035B]
  • Adj. EBITDA: $970-985M (+43-45% y/y) [from $960-990M]

with management highlighting a weakening consumer spending environment in core verticals -

In Q3 and early Q4 we experienced modestly worsening same-store-sales from our restaurant and hotel customers, but increased contribution from new verticals and geographies. As we’ve said in the past, while not immune to consumer spending levels, our massive cross-sell funnel affords us more resiliency during periods of economic uncertainty.

- Taylor Lauber, Shift4 CEO, in Q3 2025 shareholder letter.

As you may know, Shift4 acquired Global Blue to significantly expand its global reach and enhance its integrated payment offerings, particularly in luxury retail and travel sectors. Now, in recent months, Mr. Market has doled out a brutal punishment on Shift4's stock ever since the Global Blue acquisition was announced; however, this particular transaction has diversified Shift4's business mix—categorically and geographically—with exposure to the luxury retail sector reducing business volatility tied to consumer spending.

With Global Blue, Shift4 now has the opportunity to cross-sell to thousands of luxury retail customers. Now, while the bigger luxury brands like Louis Vuitton (OTCPK:LVMUY) are well-served by the likes of Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY) and Stripe (i.e., taking market share will be an uphill battle), Shift4 could certainly win several boutique firms on Global Blue's customer roster. As such, I am excited to follow this piece of the business in 2026.

As of Q3 2025, Shift4's contracted volume backlog stood at $35B, and its cross-sell payments funnel has expanded to >$1T. Over the medium term (3-4 years), Shift4 is targeting 30% CAGR growth in GRLNF and adj. EBITDA, with annual free cash flow projected to exceed $1B+ by 2028-29.

From a business standpoint, Shift4 has market-leading products in multiple categories. With international expansion still in early stages, Shift4's profitable growth story has many chapters left.

The Balance Sheet Concerns Are Overblown

Over recent quarters, Shift4 has advanced its international expansion plans via an aggressive M&A strategy that has also seen it acquire the likes of Vectron (Germany, Europe), Eigen (Canada), and SmartPay (New Zealand, Australia) in addition to the Global Blue acquisition.

As a result, Shift4's financial debt has ballooned to $4.8 billion as of the end of Q3 2025, which is roughly 5x 2025E adjusted EBITDA of $978M. Now, while this elevated leverage position is less than ideal due to higher interest costs, it is certainly not untenable.

For starters, Shift4 is a highly cash flow-generative business (FY-25E FCF: $488M, FY-28E FCF: $1B+), and the plan for Global Blue does not involve legacy business deprecation like past acquisitions, i.e., Global Blue's cash generation will support Shift4's future debt repayment efforts.

Now, with each M&A transaction, we have integration risks. And these risks are especially elevated in the Shift4-Global Blue combo due to cultural differences. However, Shift4's 27+ year history is laden with several M&A transactions, and I trust Jared Isaacman (now Executive Chairman), Taylor Lauber (CEO), and the Shift4 executive leadership team to deliver success once again!

Importantly, they have ample time to get things right. Having retired its 2026 Convertible Senior Notes [$450M] in Q2 2025 using some of the proceeds of its $3.3B capital raise, Shift4's debt repayment maturities are now staggered farther into the future.

Yes, Shift4 has a $690M debt repayment due in December 2025; the company should be able to cover it with cash (avoiding dilution from the converts). And, looking beyond that, Shift4 is expected to generate >$1B in annual FCF by 2028-29, which will be more than adequate to meet its longer-dated debt obligations.

Now, Shift4's recently-closed acquisition of SmartPay (closed on 4th November 2025, deal size ~$180M) and impending acquisition of Bambora (expected close Q1 2026, deal size unknown) aren't factored into the math here today; however, as I see it, Shift4's current liquidity and robust free cash flow generation provide adequate support for management's aggressive M&A push.

And Shift4's management and BOD seem to agree. After all, Shift4 just announced a new $1B share repurchase plan, focused on managing dilution.

At ~$73.18 per share (or $7.29B), Shift4 is trading at ~15x 2025E FCF, which is dirt cheap for a company growing as fast as Shift4. As such, I think the new stock buyback program makes total sense and should instill a high floor for FOUR stock.

Shift4 Fair Value And Expected Return

According to TQI's Valuation Model, FOUR stock is worth ~$136 per share, which is roughly 86% above its current level.

Additional Notes:

1) For the model, I have considered a $4.23B revenue base (2025E revenue) and assumed a reasonable 15% CAGR sales growth over the 2026-30 5-year modeling period.

2) While Shift4's FY-25E adj. FCF of $488M represents an adj. FCF margin of ~11.5%, I am sticking with our optimized FCF margin assumption of 15% due to an expectation of operational leverage and improved cash flow conversion for Shift4 at a larger business scale in the future.

3) Furthermore, to account for idiosyncratic risks tied to the integration of multiple M&A transactions (and consequent leverage position), I have increased the model's required IRR from 15% to 20%—thereby instilling a margin of safety in the model.

4) The share count has been raised to 99.6M shares, factoring in Shift4's $1B mandatory Convertible Series A Preferred Stock (FOUR.PR.A).

Based on a reasonable exit multiple of 20x P/FCF, I see Shift4 stock rising to $277.51 per share over the next five years at a CAGR rate of 30.55%.

With its 5-year expected CAGR return being significantly higher than TQI's investment hurdle rate of 15%, I continue to view Shift4 as an asymmetric investment for long-term investors at current levels.

FOUR Technical Setup

From a technical standpoint, Shift4 stock appears to be forming a massive triangle pattern. Now, triangles can break in either direction; however, with FOUR stocks rebounding off of the multi-year uptrend line (marked in green arrow) in recent days and weekly RSI levels indicating "oversold" conditions, I hold a constructive technical view on Shift4 stock.

Also, as per Elliot Wave Theory, Shift4 appears to have completed a 5-wave move to the downside when it tested the long-term support trendline in the low-$60s. Consequently, we can expect a move back up to the "wave-4" high around ~$90 per share in FOUR in the near-term.

And this constructive technical view is supported by Shift4's healthy business momentum and significant stock undervaluation.

Now, given heightened macroeconomic uncertainties, I remain open-minded here, but I really do like the odds of a bullish move.

Final Thoughts

Shift4 is primed for several years of rapid, profitable growth. The stock is deeply undervalued, and while its technical setup is directionally unclear, FOUR stock is sitting right above its multi-year bullish trendline. On the back of a ~40% dip off its early 2025 highs, I view Shift4's long-term risk/reward as truly asymmetric once again.

Key Takeaway: I continue to rate Shift4 a "Strong Buy" in the $70s.

As always, thank you for reading, and happy investing. Please feel free to share any questions, concerns, or thoughts in the comments section below.

data.title: Hemisphere Energy: Strong Balance Sheet, Weak Oil Environment
data.description:
Hemisphere Energy (OTCQX:HMENF) remains one of the few interesting microcap Canadian oil stocks. I like it as it maintains its positive cash flow even, when oil prices are down. Yet, since it's Q1 2025, it showed that the financial model is still functioning, but its resilience is decreasing.

The first signs that the market was changing were clear in spring - revenue growth moderated, realized prices improved to C$79/boe from C$74 a year ago, but the company was hit by expensive polymers, which are needed for enhanced oil recovery technology. This leads me to doubt dividend sustainability, and I estimate that every additional $5 change in WTI price can directly determine whether the company maintains excess FCF or just breaks even. As of Q2 netbacks were weaker at C$42.77/boe, and the realized price was C$70.06/boe. I believe that Q3 will show even more pressure, and it can affect stock price negatively.

Liquidity seems healthy. The company does not have debt and has around C$14 million, and a C$35 million credit line remains unused. This gives flexibility, which lets them not only sustain the dividend program, but also survive fluctuating oil prices. That is why, I like it; it has conservative balance, and it does make it different from other Canadian micro-E&P competitors, which most of the time rely on credit. At the same time, this safety has a price, as Hemisphere could avoid investments and aggressive drilling network expansion, when free cash flow is not secured with better prices.

Structurally, financial situation is dependent on one basin—Atlee Buffalo, which makes up around 97% of total production. While, yes, it gives operational effectiveness and cost control, it limits diversification. One serious technological or supply chain disruption (for example, polymer injection chain) could briefly knock out an entire company's cash flow. In 2024-2025, polymer flood activity increased operating costs, which is decreasing netbacks. This factor, combined with weaker realized prices (~70 CAD/bbl vs. 80 CAD/bbl in 2024), might put pressure on FCF yield.

The dividend program remains the main management priority. Yearly base payout (C$0.10 per share), I think, is still sustainable even at WTI being $60, but to be fair, I doubt that special dividends will continue. Management is signalling, that any additional capital distribution depends on WTI staying above $70. While the share buyback program is still ongoing, I feel it might slow down (although they have bought around 1.3 million shares).

If we look at a wider view, Hemisphere's financial situation reflects typical low leverage but cyclical sensitivity. The company has enough capital to survive at least 12-18 months with lower commodity prices, but I don't see them growing until the WTI price jumps back up. The biggest danger is not losing its liquidity, but long-term cash flow shrinking, which would force them to decrease dividends.

All in all, financially the company is still healthy, but sensitive. It has all the signs of a stable company, but its profitability buffer evaporates together with oil prices. Until WTI reaches $70 again, I believe they are going to stand in defensive positions - more of a stability story, not a growth story.

Assets

Atlee Buffalo

Hemisphere operations depend on one concentrated asset - Atlee Buffalo basin in southeast Alberta. Like I mentioned, this is not only the company’s core, but its financial foundation. Around 97% of production is made here, and Hemisphere has 100% operational and property rights, so it is controlling capital distribution and technology decisions.

Atlee Buffalo is made out of two pools, Upper Mannville F and G pools. Polymer EOR project success made Atlee Buffalo a cash-flow factory. The company basically works in a harvest regime: investments in drilling are going down, and a bigger part of FCF is directed to dividends and share buybacks. Nevertheless, project needs maintenance; polymers need to be supplied regularly. As I said before, polymer flood activity increased operating costs in 2025.

Atlee Buffalo has almost 10 million barrels of proved (1P) reserves, and 2P reserves are over 14.5 million barrels. Proved Developed Producing reaches more than 9 million boe with quite low additional capital (C$16 million). This shows that the project is mature, has low sustaining capital needs, and is a stable, free cash flow generating asset. Why am I saying that this is a long-life-span asset? As with the current tempo of 3800 boe/d, the reserves should last 8-10 years. From what I have seen in the market, similar companies have around 5-7 years.

Another important part is that due to EOR technology, big part of 2P reserves has the potential to become PDP category reserves without significant investments. Due to this conversion ratio, I believe Hemisphere has a competitive advantage. When a company can maintain reservoir pressure and increase recovery rate, the reserves not only increase in lifespan but also become even more cash flow generating.

Marsden Project

This one is a pilot expansion zone. Five wells were drilled (two injectors and three production wells), and a new injection facility was also built. This is an experimental project that lets Hemisphere test polymer flooding effectiveness in a new geological environment. Even though Marsden makes up a few percent of the company's reserves, a successful project could give Hemisphere a second leg - a second production center and additional reserve base. They expect a reaction to the injection by the end of 2025, and I am looking forward to it, as it might become a new catalyst.

Hemisphere has very few assets, as you can see, but at least I expect that it is profitable when WTI is above $65-70. One basin means less diversification. On the other hand, polymeric technology is a double-edged sword; it gives lifespan, but requires stability of raw material supply and also capital discipline. The company is very concentrated, and this is a major red flag I have when oil prices are fluctuating.

Capital Effectiveness And Dividends

One of the reasons why the company can generate quite stable cash flow even in fluctuating oil is due to F&D price, which is C$1.9-2.1/boe, when in the market I know a lot of companies that have it around C$6-8/boe. This shows that Atlee Buffalo Basin has a high return coefficient - low well density, good recovery rate, and low infrastructure costs let them have a strong return on invested capital.

The company’s sustaining CapEx is just C$4-6 million a year, which is around 20-25% AFF. Structure like this is exceptionally effective, as Hemisphere can finance dividends. Capital is distributed only to maintaining production and polymer injection effectiveness, not expansion.

Dividends

This is the part where I see a lot of risk. Currently they are sustainable, but their future belongs to WTI dynamics. If it stays at $60/bbl, base C$0.10 annual payout should still be covered, but I estimate that the coverage ratio decreased to 1.5-2x. If WTI prices fell to $55 or lower (which might happen, given that while I write this article, WTI is -4% on the daily chart), financing dividends from free cash flow would become difficult. Also, I believe special dividends might be paused.

In short, current dividends stand on effectiveness, not growth. This means that Hemisphere is a defensive income play, which will continue paying out, as long as FCF remains positive, but any long-term WTI price fall might erase the financial buffer. Also, check out the Seeking Alpha warning.

Valuation

Hemisphere Energy today is valued moderately. EV/EBITDA (TTM) is around 3.4x, when Gear Energy (KGEI) is valued at 4.09x, and Headwater Exploration (OTCQX:ALVOF) - 4.11x. This shows, that market values Hemisphere with around a 20-25% discount, even though the balance is strong. PE (TTM) at around 6.5x, compared to 7.8x for KGEI and 8.4x for ALVOF, EV/Sales (FWD) - 1.95x, lower than most of the competitors.

Price to Cash Flow (TTM) reaches 3.45x, when Gear Energy is 4.73x or Evolution Petroleum (EPM) at 4.73. This proves that Hemisphere is valued as a cash-efficient and low growth company, but overall valuation is quite moderate, and I would even say cheap given its sustainable dividend position.

Summary

Hemisphere Energy remains financially healthy, but very cyclical and sensitive. The strengths are its strong balance sheet, low CapEx need, and sustainable dividends, which might still be covered even at WTI $60. Main asset Atlee Buffalo generates stable cash flows, but dependency on one asset and polymer pricing are the main risk.

My position remains hold, it is attractive for investors that are searching for a dividend defensive position with low financial risk, but it's not suitable for those looking for growth. Also, even for dividend investing, it's risky and should be looked at as a speculation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dell: From AI Hype To Real Margins - $180 In Sight
data.description:
The stock's upside is in margins, not servers

My concern with Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) isn't how many AI services it can sell, but how much it can earn (margins). Ultimately, as we all know, it's profits that drive stocks up or down, not volumes (of servers, in this case). A turning point could come if the server and infrastructure division (called the "ISG" division) manages to achieve stable and consistent operating margins between 10 and 14 percent in the coming quarters. For me, it remains the true long-term growth driver.

What will you find in this article?

In this article, I'll analyze:

  • How much AI-related orders, such as those from Microsoft or IREN, are truly creating value for Dell, and how much they are eroding margins.
  • I'll analyze the "attach" component (i.e., how much storage and services it manages to sell alongside AI servers), the effect of value engineering on Blackwell systems, and what all this means for prices and profitability. My goal is to analyze whether AI is driving profits or just revenue.
  • Then we'll move on to valuation, because Dell is still priced at low multiples today.
  • I'll highlight the aspects I'm interested in seeing in the quarterly results at the end of November. My focus will be on: a) gross margin trends, b) ISG division margins, c) the mix of enterprise and sovereign customers, and d) the outlook for next year.
  • Risks and opportunities will close this analysis. We'll discuss data center energy availability, white-box pricing pressure, and PC-side execution.
  • For those who follow the stock, this could be an interesting in-depth analysis to read. As always, please give me your feedback in the comments and tell me what you think of the company, its stock positioning, and its prospects.

Latest quarterly report

I​‍​‌‍​‍‌ won't get into it too much here; the last quarterly report was published in August, and we are a lot closer to the next one that will be released on the 25th of this month. I'll just give a brief rundown of the things that were very successful and those that were ​‍​‌‍​‍‌not.

The numbers published in the quarterly report are truly impressive. There's no point in reporting each figure. I'll tell you what I think. Right now, artificial intelligence has finally become one of the main drivers of company revenue. Orders and shipments were impressive in terms of overall revenue. For more information, I recommend carefully reading the report.

In the call with analysts, management emphasized that the pipeline is far greater than the current order book, so we're not talking about imaginary orders but actual demand that has already been satisfied. Systems like the GB200, NVL72, and GB300 are in operation.

The ISG division I mentioned in the introduction to this article (the one that hosts AI servers and infrastructure) has grown significantly, up 44%, which is undoubtedly significant. As an analyst, I'm not going to be impressed or enamored by these numbers; we need to see continuity, and, above all, what matters now is no longer the speed of volumes but the quality of margins. As long as Dell continues to sell AI servers with compressed profit profiles, growth will remain more "industrial" than "financial". Great for revenue, less so for value generation.

If the company manages to stabilize its ISG margin above 10% in the coming quarters and grow its storage and services component alongside its servers, then yes, I'd feel more at ease.

Margins remain a work in progress. Gross margin remained more or less unchanged, although as a percentage of revenue, it fell to 21.4% (from 22% in the previous quarter). One-time costs for expediting and reconfiguration likely weighed negatively on Blackwell.

What I want to see in the next quarterly report

In the next quarterly report, I need to see a couple of things materialize. Above all, I want to see margins recover. So far, Dell hasn't given in to the temptation to cut prices just to boost volume. On the contrary, it has defended its price list, and this has begun to be reflected in the financial statements. Now we need to see how the situation evolves, whether for better or worse.

The other important point is that the storage and services "attachment" (i.e., the accessory sold with servers) is becoming automatic, no longer tied to individual projects or customized offerings. This means more recurring revenues and more stable margins. It's the part that, if properly integrated, can create continuity and long-term visibility.

Finally, it's becoming clear that the healthiest demand comes from the enterprise and sovereign world, not just from the large hyperscalers (Microsoft, Amazon, etc.). This is an important aspect, because the enterprise and sovereign world guarantees Dell a certain continuity and pricing power, while the latter brings huge volumes but often slim margins.

I'm interested to know if Blackwell's value engineering is actually affecting costs and if the supply chain has changed from "chasing demand at all costs" to a "structured and sustainable" strategy.

Valuation

I'll start with EPS, as always. Expectations for the coming years are as follows.

EPS for 2026 is $9.55, and I don't make any specific additional comments on this number. The key is to understand where Dell can naturally reach without any particular scenarios or sudden changes. For FY27, EPS is $11.3 (source: Bloomberg Consensus), and I don't have much to say here either, because this figure can be reached simply by removing one-off items and improving the mix toward enterprise and sovereign customers (as I described earlier) and with some value engineering at Blackwell, which lowers the cost per rack. So far, it's just normalization. Time will be your best ally.

In my opinion, the market continues to underestimate two important levers:

  • structural attachment
  • and buybacks.

"Structural​‍​‌‍​‍‌ attach" refers to the practice of consistently and automatically coupling storage, networking, and services with hardware that is sold, rather than just in special projects. So, Business continuity. Long-term visibility. No surprises. ​‍​That's what it means for me. If this integration becomes structural, it will change the quality of the business, and every additional percentage point of attachment translates into higher margins, more predictable revenues, and less dependence on hardware cycles.

Then comes the question of capital. Dell vowed in its earnings presentation to share over 80% of its free cash flow with its stockholders, as you can see here.

At these prices, if the pace of buybacks remains similar, a 3–4% annual increase in basic EPS due solely to the lower number of shares outstanding is entirely realistic.

Combining the two levers—i.e., higher revenues from services and storage and a regular buyback—I arrive at a 2027 EPS of around $12 per share.

From there, the choice is all about the multiple. I've never fallen in love with individual stocks, and this won't be the case either. In my opinion, with a P/E of 15x, the stock reflects healthy growth and significant cash generation. This isn't fantasy, but realistic, just above the tech sector median (source: Seeking Alpha). This value recognizes the company's potential in terms of artificial intelligence when it translates into real profits, not just volumes.

So we said $12 EPS and a 15x multiple. The fair price, according to my valuation, is $180 per share.

I'm changing my approach for the ultimate test. I need to be sure of what I'm doing.

  • If I stay closer to the analyst consensus ($11.3), at 15x I get $170 per share.
  • If the overall market slows down, then I pay 14 times, and with $12 I get $168 per share;
  • With $11.3, we get $158.

To be completely honest, the true operational bear case for me is 13x on $11.3 (a scenario that occurs if the mix doesn't improve and the attach is weak). In that case, the price is $146.

Risks

So far, I've highlighted the positive aspects and therefore the main opportunities. As always, no investment is risk-free, and in my opinion, the main one is margins, not revenues. My main concern, in fact, is the new Blackwell/NVL72 racks, which remain expensive to assemble, meaning the company could experience flat margin growth, favoring higher revenues and volumes. To overcome this limitation, the company must implement a solid cost optimization strategy (value engineering, to be clear); otherwise, it risks continuing to grow revenues but with stagnant margins. Shareholders aren't interested in sales but in how many dollars are earned from each sale.

Another risk concerns AI-related demand, which can be uneven. Dell has a massive backlog, but deliveries depend on practical factors such as power availability, cooling systems, and customer installation times. If any of these factors slow down, even for just a few weeks, it could be enough to result in a weaker-than-expected quarter.

Margins, cash, and a $180 reality check

The opportunities are there, especially at these prices, especially after a stock price reversal.

Dell has to concentrate on turning artificial intelligence into a real driver not only of revenue but also—and most especially—of margin. Clearly the first element that could be helpful in this case is "attach", as I said before.

Then, the situation in the capital is very favorable. The last earnings say Dell is a company that will probably repay its shareholders over 80% of the adjusted free cash flow through dividends and buybacks. Therefore, any operational improvement in ISG will lead to a very rapid increase in EPS, thus bringing the revaluation point closer to my $180.

I'll add a detail that the market is ignoring or not considering adequately, in my opinion. The​‍​‌‍​‍‌ deal with IREN and Microsoft (that comprises GB300 components, networking, and services) is not just a source of increased revenue, but it also implies an additional $0.30-$0.40 of EPS that is quite significant on a yearly ​‍​‌‍​‍‌basis.

At these levels, I see a favorable risk/reward ratio for anyone entering at this level. Personally, I've been in the market longer (although this is the first time I've written about Dell on this platform).

data.title: 2 Years Of The Rise Of The 'Magnificent Seven' Tech Stocks
data.description:
The seven companies that make up the S&P 500's "Magnificent Seven" stocks have been on the rise for some time.

Two years ago, they combined to account for 30% of the total valuation of the S&P 500 (SPX). And to be sure, several of the members of this elite group of high-flying companies were already among the biggest components of the index before they became collectively known as the "Magnificent 7". The weren't known by that label until 2023, when Bank of American analyst Michael Hartnett first deployed the phrase with respect to "a basket of seven stocks: Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA)".

Since then, these mega-cap stocks have earned the moniker by growing even larger. Through October 2025, they have collectively grown to account for 37% of the entire market capitalization of the S&P 500.

Pretty amazing stuff when you realize that Tesla wasn't even a publicly-traded company until 2010 and Nvidia's explosive growth because of its Artificial Intelligence (AI) enabling computer chip technology began in 2023.

The following pair of charts illustrates the growth of the Magnificent Seven stocks over the past two years. The first chart shows how their value has increased and driven the growth of the S&P 500 index itself. The second chart shows how their collective share of the S&P 500 index has likewise grown.

They're able to drive the total valuation of the S&P 500 because their market capitalizations make up such a large share of the index. In today's market, what happens to the S&P 500 index is almost all about what's happening with the Magnificent Seven stocks.

How long might that last? Time will tell, but it helps put today's record concentration of market capitalization by seven companies into some context, the last time the S&P 500 index (or rather, its predecessor index, the S&P 90) had such a large percentage of its total value represented by such a small number of stocks was back in 1932.

References

Standard & Poor's. S&P Market Attributes Web File. [Excel Spreadsheet]. Accessed 3 November 2025.

Original Post

data.title: Forget The AI Race: Build Income First To Survive The Next Market Crash
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Investors are living in constant fear of the next recession. They are right, there will be another recession. The problem is, we never know when a recession is going to happen. Just like if you live in certain parts of the country, you know a hurricane is going to happen, and in others, you know there will be a tornado. It isn't a question of "if", it's a question of when the next one will occur, how close it will be, and how bad it will be.

For the past decade, I'd say the question I have been asked the most is "What if there is a recession?" I will sometimes pontificate on when the next recession might be, what might cause it, what it might look like, and other details. Yet, as I was sitting there buying up stocks in 2019, the risk of COVID-19 wasn't something that I even considered.

I'll go so far as to say that if I wrote an article talking about the danger of a worldwide pandemic causing entire economies to shut down, that we would all be confined to our homes until further notice, and that entire industries would come to a screeching halt, the good editors at Seeking Alpha would have referred me to a good Sci-Fi publisher.

The harsh reality of investing is that if you've fooled yourself into believing you know the future, then you're fooling yourself. We can make educated guesses, but even the most educated of guesses are still guesses.

To The Market, Ignorance Is Bliss

The market remains high with large-cap valuations now higher than they have been since 2001. Source.

Small and mid-cap companies continue to trade at moderate valuations.

We saw a pretty unanimous story from several mega-caps. Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Apple Inc. (AAPL) all announced that they will be spending a lot more on CapEx for their AI programs. The AI arms race is on – for these companies, growing AI is both an opportunity and an essential to remain relevant.

For now, the market is happy to let them shovel capital to burn in the engine, with the hope that it will eventually produce the strong returns that are demanded at these valuations.

Valuations are high because the market is expecting some significant LTEG (Long-term Expected Growth) of 17%.

This is a measure of the 5-year growth rate based on consensus estimates of operating earnings.

While there are certainly some companies where one can argue that +17% growth for five years is a reasonable estimate, is the S&P 500 average going to be that high?

No.

That's the assumption that is driving the market. And the market will keep its foot on the gas pedal until reality smacks it across the face. We've seen this movie before.

I'm sure this time is different.

Our Strategy

Our strategy is to keep a level head. We aren't going to go overpay for stocks because their prices are high. We are going to remain focused on identifying companies we like that are trading at attractive valuations. We buy, hold, and generate the lion's share of our return in dividends.

I wouldn't be terribly surprised if we see the stock market continue to trade at all-time highs for the next six months. People are very enthusiastic about the potential for AI, and they are willing to pay any amount of money to be part of it. As long as that remains true, valuations will be high in that fairly sizable portion of the market.

I also won't be surprised if the market tanks and we see a 50% decline in the S&P 500. Ultimately, the stock market is dependent upon new buyers having cash to buy. New money coming in is what keeps prices going up. People who can't feed their families will stop investing money. People who lose their jobs stop investing. No matter how great the outlook for a company is, investors have to have money to buy stock, and when buyers are scarce, prices decline.

There will be a lot of money to be made in AI, and some investors are going to make a lot of money investing in it. If you want to speculate on the winners, I'm not going to discourage you. What I will say is, set up your base first. Develop a plan so that the income you need is being produced by your portfolio on a consistent and recurring basis.

What you don't want to be is one of those investors who gets in a tough personal financial position and decides to sell shares at poor prices to pay bills. It doesn't matter if you were a genius who bought Amazon in 1998, recognizing it was the next big thing, if you had to sell it at a 70% loss in 2001 to pay your bills.

Back To The Future

Consider this: it is January 1999, there is a young investor, we'll call him Marty, and we only have 30 seconds to give him investment advice. Marty only has $10,000 to open up his very first brokerage account. He can buy Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY), or both. Coming from the future, which would you recommend this young person buy?

Obviously, if Marty can buy and hold forever, coming from the future, AMZN is the "right" choice. Sure, it was "expensive" but a $10,000 investment in AMZN in January 1999 would be worth about $934k today. An absolutely incredible return that blows away the S&P 500, the Nasdaq, and other indices.

That is, if Marty can buy & hold forever, no matter what.

What if he can't? It's a recession; there's a fair chance Marty is going to lose his job, and that old Geo Metro he's been driving around in might break down. Marty might need to save $2,000 to buy a new (used) car. Not a problem because Marty was a responsible young man who worked hard to save up $10,000 for his brokerage and invested it in what the person from the future said was the best investment. He can just sell a few shares to buy the new car!

Or all the shares, as it turned out. Marty's $10,000 investment in one of the best stocks of all time turned into an 80% loss because Marty prioritized being able to drive to work.

Bills Don't Stop

This isn't an idle consideration. Marty is a fictional character, but I knew people who were forced sellers during the Dot-com bust or the GFC (Great Financial Crisis) for various reasons. Sure, some were just voluntary trading mistakes. People got scared and sold even when they didn't "need" to. It is mentally very hard to see an 80% loss. Yet, others simply had very real needs for the money. Bills don't stop, and emergencies don't stop happening during a recession. Income sources like employment do stop for many people – that is the definition of a recession. It isn't unusual for people to tap their 401(k)s or other investment accounts when they lose their jobs or have other unexpected financial hardships.

While it is a terrible investment move, I don't blame people who prioritize eating, shelter, and transportation today over financial impacts 20 years from now. What use is money if you can't spend it when you have an urgent need?

A Better Way

What if, instead of buying just AMZN, Marty split his investment in half? Half to AMZN and half to NLY for the income?

According to Portfolio Visualizer, a 50/50 mix portfolio of AMZN and NLY bought on January 1st, 1999, would have had a value of $12,321 by December 2001 for his new (used) car. Marty could have withdrawn the profit and left $10,000 invested to grow more. In fact, the portfolio was producing $1,089/year in dividends, so if Marty could find a way to make payments on a vehicle, he wouldn't even need to sell any shares.

But what about his future retirement?

A 50/50 Mix portfolio of NLY and AMZN outperformed a portfolio of AMZN alone. Marty had the income when/if he needed it, and even though he diversified away from one of the best individual stocks of all time, he had a higher total return.

The reason is simple. NLY outperformed at times that AMZN underperformed, and AMZN outperformed at times that NLY underperformed.

Their correlation is 0.08, which means that they aren't correlated at all. That's a good thing for ensuring you have an all-terrain portfolio. NLY's ability to produce cash flow benefited the portfolio because it produced cash to buy more AMZN when prices were dirt cheap. Plus, it offers the side benefit of producing cash flow, if Marty needed it, preventing him from selling shares at low prices.

Conclusion

We're never going to offer you stocks that have +9,000% returns. If you're looking for a lottery-ticket-type winner, we don't have it at High Dividend Opportunities. We don't specialize in those types of investments. What we can provide you is a good base that provides you with the income you need to pay your bills. If you then want to take a little excess of your portfolio and invest in what you believe will be "the next big thing", great. You can do that with the knowledge and security that your bills are paid. That you can hold through whatever crazy gyrations the market goes through.

I don't know which companies are going to walk away from the AI arms race with huge returns. What I do know is that there are a lot of places in the market to buy income today, including NLY. If you set up your portfolio to have strong cash flow so you know your bills are paid, you will be able to face the adversity and volatility of the market with a much clearer head. If you then decide to speculate on who the winners of the AI arms race will be, you can do it without risking your ability to fund your retirement. That's what we seek to achieve with the Income Method.

People often talk about income investing and growth investing as if they are in opposition. The reality is that the two investment styles can complement each other. If you're going to speculate on Growth investments, trying to find the next big thing, make sure your income is covered first. Then, when you're going on that crazy ride, you can do it knowing that your bills are paid, that you have food on the table, and a roof over your head.

data.title: The Next Big Trade: Why I'm Loading Up On REITs Before Everyone Else
data.description:
Introduction

A few months ago, I had a call with an asset manager about just how tricky this macro environment has become. That may not come as a shock, as we discuss this almost daily. During this call, I jokingly mentioned that I sometimes feel like selling everything and just putting it in a high-income stock to "call it a day."

Obviously, I’m joking. I love my job and consider "death" my only exit strategy.

I didn’t come up with that, as it’s a quote I stole from a podcast and had to look up online.

Anyway, my point is that it can be very tempting to buy income. It’s something I discussed yesterday when I laid out three strategies for this market, one of which was the "I don’t know" approach for people who simply aren’t in the business of predicting the economy’s future.

For them, buying high-quality income vehicles can be a terrific move, especially when they come with ultra-safe business models. The same goes for retirees, who basically depend on generating the most income from their hard-earned dollars.

But that’s not why I’m writing this article.

This isn’t a random article where I throw some random high-income picks your way.

No, this is about the bigger picture.

In this market, I believe there are tremendous opportunities in the income space, following what Horizon Kinetics calls the Equity Yield Curve, something I discussed in a personal strategy article on Sunday, which I highly recommend to new readers.

Essentially, it’s about buying undervalued, high-quality equities with a longer-term mindset to capture market inefficiencies caused by a short-term focus among many investors.

The best thing is that the new investment regime, which I expect, could be highly beneficial for higher-yielding dividend stocks, which means I get to cover a lot of bases in this article.

It’s an opportunity that perfectly fits my new motto, which I brought up on Sunday as well:

Time is the edge, quality is the filter, and valuation is the trigger

So, let’s dive in!

Why Income is Becoming a Core Trade of the Next Cycle

Everyone knows the market has been doing well since the Great Financial Crisis. This is obviously due to earnings growth, but also due to higher valuations. As we can see below, the market traded below 12.0x forward earnings until it started to gain steam in 2012.

Now, it trades at slightly more than 20x earnings, which means multiple expansion has been a big factor, supporting mega-cap tech and related investments that now dominate the market. This has sucked a lot of air out of the market, so to speak, causing less attractive investments to suffer, at least on a relative basis.

Using Vanguard data, here are some S&P 500 weighting numbers:

  • NVIDIA (NVDA) accounts for roughly 8.0% of the index.
  • The entire energy sector accounts for 2.9%.
  • The entire utilities sector has a 2.3% weighting.
  • Real estate accounts for just 1.9%.
  • Materials have a 1.8% weighting.

I say this a lot, but think about this for a second.

This has hurt a lot of companies, including the ones I just mentioned, as neither higher yields nor solid cash flows nor relatively cheap valuations could provide investors with alpha. Again, I’m painting with a broad brush, but that’s what happened in general.

I’m allowed to paint with a broad brush because, using JPMorgan data, we see that value stocks, in general, have not stood a chance against growth stocks for at least a full decade.

That’s why Cohen & Steers now makes the case that the second half of the 2020s will look much more than the previous decades, as longer-term returns are driven by fundamentals, which remain healthy. In this case, they are talking about real estate as a sector that has returned 12.4% per year since 1991. However, since 2020, that return has dropped to a mere 5.5%, a number that pales in comparison to pretty much any major sector.

There’s plenty of evidence to make the case for a rebound and higher returns.

The Real Estate Risk/Reward is Fantastic

Real estate is cheap.

Using more data from Cohen & Steers, where I got the chart below, we see that the spread in earnings multiples between U.S. REITs and the S&P 500 has declined to pandemic lows. Even during the Great Financial Crisis, when REITs were in much worse shape than they are now, as I will show you in this article, the valuation gap wasn’t this wide.

To add more context, the chart also shows that the valuation spread is now more than 10x its long-term average. In general, REITs tend to trade at a discount, as we can clearly see (purple line).

This is very important to mention.

For example, using long-term data from Nareit (ending October 2025), REITs have returned between 6.8% and 12.0% per year using 20-50-year time horizons. Meanwhile, the S&P 500 has returned between 10.8% and 12.0% during these periods (depending on the cutoff, of course). Essentially, these ranges tell us that on a very long-term basis, the odds of generating decent returns are in favor of the S&P 500.

So, why should we buy REITs? I'm sure that's a question many of you may have right now.

One big reason is correlations (read: risk management).

While U.S. REITs are still positively correlated with the market, as we can see below, the average correlation on a 12-quarter rolling basis since 1981 is just 0.6. In other words, it's an asset that doesn't always move in lockstep with the S&P 500.

That said, to me, correlation benefits aren’t enough.

That's where benefit number two comes in, which is income.

On average, REITs yield 1.1% (110 basis points) more than the 10-year treasury yield. This means investors get a premium by buying REITs. That makes sense, as they have more risks than government bonds. Besides that, REITs can grow their dividends and generate other forms of long-term value.

While I am writing this, the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) yields 3.9%. The 10-year government bond yields 4.1%. As we can see below, since 2022, bonds have yielded higher.

The current negative premium in REIT income is mainly due to the surge in bond yields, as REIT yields have almost remained flat at 4% (with some outliers) since 2009. The chart below shows this quite clearly.

If we assume an unchanged dividend payout ratio (I present evidence in a second), it tells us that per-share FFO (funds from operations) was the main driver of total returns.

In other words, while REITs have a yield disadvantage compared to bonds right now, I believe that disadvantage will turn into an advantage, as I have often written about the Fed’s and the government’s plans to "run it hot." That thesis is based on my belief that they have to kickstart cyclical growth, get the housing market running, and support the consumer.

As I wrote in this article (among others), this will likely lead to a scenario where long-term rates are under pressure. If these yields come down, REIT income becomes relatively more attractive. This is a crucial part of my long-term thesis.

Meanwhile, the VNQ ETF has a yield premium of nearly 300 basis points over the S&P 500, as we can see below. This means a potential income-driven rotation could be even more beneficial for REITs.

On top of that, we can assume REIT dividends will remain stable. This, too, is crucial, as there is no point in making the case for a relative income tailwind for REITs if their dividends aren’t safe.

Using the latest Nareit data, we find that the average REIT dividend payout is roughly 80%. That number has been unchanged for decades, with an outlier during the Great Financial Crisis when many REITs had way too much leverage and were forced to cut.

To give you more evidence of a healthier balance sheet, we see (below) that total debt as a percentage of the market value of REIT assets has been close to 35% for more than a decade. During the Great Financial Crisis, it was briefly at 65% due to a higher base going into the recession and a steep decline in asset prices.

Moreover, the equity-to-total assets ratio is above 40%. That number was below 34% in 2008.

And then there’s net operating income growth, which has been elevated since the pandemic.

In other words, I think it’s fair to say that the main reason that caused REITs to underperform in recent years is the fact that government bond yields offered more income and lower risks.

Now, that may be reversing.

Moreover, REITs are not all the same. This, too, is a critical part of the thesis.

Using the latest data, we see that on a year-to-date basis, the all-REIT index returned 2.2%. Depending on what index one uses, equity REITs return somewhere between 2% and 3%.

Outliers are office REITs, which are down 8%; residential REITs, which are down 12%; lodging REITs, which are down 14%; and other smaller niche REITs.

In other words, while it’s perfectly fine to approach REITs as an asset class, it is crucial to understand that the group, as a whole, is very diverse and prone to various drivers.

To give you a few examples of stocks on my radar:

  • Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) is a biotech-focused REIT that suffers from increasing oversupply in the healthcare space.
  • Rexford Industrial (REXR), which I own, has an asset base of warehouses and industrial properties that is 100% focused on Southern California. Trade war fears (SoCal has a major port complex) and post-pandemic supply growth hit it hard.
  • Strip mall REITs like Kimco (KIM) benefitted from very low supply growth and strong demand from brick-and-mortar stores, causing an unexpected tailwind.

Personally, I have turned bullish on some of the "losers" of the past cycle, as I have been an aggressive buyer of Rexford on weakness. I’m a fan of its much larger peer, Prologis (PLD), and I’m even a supporter of buying extreme weakness in cold-storage stocks like Lineage (LINE) and Americold (COLD), which have suffered from destocking and oversupply.

Lineage, for example, trades at a mere 10.6x adjusted FFO with a 6% yield, a 63% payout ratio, and analyst estimates of bottoming per-share AFFO in 2026.

This is definitely a stock more speculative investors may enjoy.

Investors who enjoy safety will likely end up benefiting more from Realty Income (O), which may be the most well-known REIT on the market. That’s why I won’t have to tell you much about it, except that it yields 5.7% with a 76% payout ratio and a risk/reward that perfectly embodies the entire REIT situation.

As we can see above, the company is expected to grow its per-share AFFO by roughly 3% going forward. If we add its 5.7% yield, we get a total return outlook of roughly 9% per year.

This is simply math by adding its per-share growth to its yield. Assuming the company has no valuation tailwinds (or headwinds), we can assume its stock price will rise in line with its per-share AFFO growth.

Now comes the interesting part, which is that Realty Income trades at just 13.4x AFFO. Its long-term average is 17.2x. In other words, it perfectly embodies the "cheapness" of REITs, which means even slight valuation tailwinds could provide much higher returns.

And while I’m at it, I may as well give you more ideas.

I’m also looking into FrontView REIT (FVR), which is a micro version of Realty Income with a market cap of less than $300 million. It yields 6.6% with a 71% payout ratio and owns 319 properties across 37 states with a 98% occupancy rate and 33% income from investment-grade tenants.

I really like its focus on smaller assets in high-demand areas and its ability to develop concepts with strong anchor tenants and increasing traffic.

I don’t own it due to my focus on growth, but if I had an income-oriented portfolio, I would have made it a part of my portfolio, as I think its 11x AFFO valuation, high yield, and 4-6% annual per-share AFFO growth (FactSet) make for a terrific risk/reward.

Another one I have to mention is Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS). It’s a REIT I have liked for years. While it yields just 3.3%, I love its business model, as it owns manufactured housing and RV communities, as well as marinas. Most of its tenants own their own homes, which means it simply collects lot fees (or annual subscriptions in the case of RV plots) and takes care of its assets and amenities.

Major secular growth tailwinds include the ongoing retirement wave, housing affordability issues, and increasing supply tailwinds from low MH community building rates.

I would put this in a "REIT growth" basket.

Areas I am not too fond of are healthcare, due to political and tenant cost risks (I expect healthcare expenses to bite many operators), and I’m also careful in the data center space.

For these areas, I prefer to invest in non-REIT data center companies that may be less exposed to potential overbuilding in the years ahead.

This isn’t a warning, but just what’s going on in my head right now.

That said, when it comes to unlocking value, Cohen & Steers notes that once REITs get to a discount of 2.0x or greater compared to the S&P 500, they tend to report substantially higher returns on at least a 1- and 2-year basis, as we can see below.

This brings me to the next part of this article.

Takeaway

Having said all of this, the situation is quite favorable.

  • REITs have a clear income advantage over the S&P 500, as they yield 3.8% (VNQ ETF) versus the market’s 1.1% yield.
  • The bullet point alone isn’t a tailwind. However, when adding the expected pressure on (longer-term) rates, that advantage starts to grow, as it will create more demand for high-quality income.
  • REITs are growing consistently. While there are clear differences between REIT subsectors, overall financial health is terrific. Moreover, most are well-protected against AI disruption.
  • REIT valuations are trading at such a big relative advantage that even a small rotation could offer tremendous value.

We could even narrow it down to three areas, which are consistently rising income with an increasing advantage over the market, supportive FFO growth, and a high chance of valuation mean reversion.

I am not, by any means, saying that REITs will skyrocket tomorrow.

What I am saying here is that, going forward, the risk/reward for this group of stocks is really attractive, making it one of the best places to buy income. At these levels, investors aren’t just buying income and potential dividend growth but also a shot at a return to double-digit annual total returns.

That's why I will use REITs to diversify my portfolio in the months and quarters ahead, as I think the risk/reward is hard to beat.

Risks to My Thesis

Risks include a re-acceleration in long-term rates, which would almost guarantee REIT underperformance if the last few years have been any indication. Another risk is a full-blown recession. While I expect many REITs to have no problems surviving the next recession, it could lead to stock price declines.

To me, the biggest risks are property-specific risks. This includes issues like overstocking in cold storage, overbuilding in industrial real estate, cost issues among health care tenants, and other issues.

Then there’s the duration trap. Even if REITs continue to do well, they may still be treated like simply interest rate proxies, which brings me back to the first risk I just mentioned.

That said, if we focus on the right assets, I think my motto makes perfect sense here:

"Time is the edge, quality is the filter, and valuation is the trigger."

data.title: U.S. Dollar Remains Soft Despite Disappointing U.K. Growth And Eurozone Industrial Output
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar (DXY) is trading heavier against most of the world's currencies today. Better-than-expected Australian employment data, which boosts speculation that the central bank's easing cycle is over, helps explain the Australian dollar's gain to its best level of the month. However, sterling is firm and extended yesterday's gains despite news that the economy contracted in September and barely grew at all in Q3. September industrial output in the eurozone disappointed but the euro reached $1.1635, a new high for the month as well. Emerging market currencies are also firmer, but for a few East Asian currencies and the Turkish lira.

Equities are mostly higher. China's CSI 300 (SHSZ300) rose 1.2% to lead the regional rally. Only Taiwan and Australia among the larger bourses failed to advance. Europe's STOXX 600 (STOXX) is trading with a firmer bias, with a three-day rally in tow. US index futures (SPX) are largely steady. European benchmark 10-year yields are narrowly mixed, and the 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is little changed near 4.07%. The US Treasury will sell $205 bln in bills and $25 bln 30-year bonds today. Four Fed officials speak, but at least four officials who have spoken recently seem to favor standing pat next month. Gold continues to recover. It is higher for the fifth consecutive session today and reached $4240, the highest since October 21 when it traded to $4375 (record high set the previous day ~$4381.50). December WTI has steadied after plunging nearly 4.2% following heightened concerns about oversupply. It initially extended yesterday's decline to almost $58 before steadying. Net-net, it is off about a dollar from last week's settlement (~$59.75).

USD: The Dollar Index snapped a five-day downdraft yesterday but has been sold to a new low for the month today near 99.15. It has frayed the 20-day moving average (~99.30) but has not settled below it since September 23. Support is seen in the 98.75-99.00 band. The daily momentum indicators are still falling. The longest government shutdown in US history is over. Arguably, part of the reason shutdowns have recurred is that the economic fallout tends to be minimal, even if the disruption of lives is real. The rule of thumb is that every week the closure costs about 0.1% of GDP. The Atlanta Fed GDP tracker had GDP rising by 4% in Q3. When the economy reopens, it typically recoups the loss growth quickly. October CPI was initially due today, but it may never be seen. The September employment report, though, which was due on October 7, could be released early next week. Recall that according to ADP, private sector employment fell by 29k, the second consecutive monthly decline.

Euro: Yesterday, the euro mostly traded in about a third-of-a-cent range below $1.1600 yesterday. Options for 1.5 bln euros at $1.1590 expire later today. Another batch for 1.25 bln euros at $1.1575 also expires today. It posted its highest settlement this month, near $1.1595. It has risen to $1.1635 today, which approached the (38.2%) decline since the year's high on September 17 near $1.1920. Nearby resistance is seen in the $1.1640-50 area. Industrial output in the eurozone rose by a disappointing 0.2% (the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey was of a 0.7% recovery after contracting 1.2% in August). On a workday-adjusted basis, industrial output rose 1.2% year-over-year (2.0% expected). The manufacturing PMI has improved every month this year but September, ironically, when it slipped to 49.8 from 50.7 in August. Tomorrow, the eurozone is expected to confirm that aggregate economy grew by 0.2% in Q3 after 0.1% in Q2 (and 0.6% in Q1). EMU's September trade balance will also be reported. Through August, the trade surplus averaged 13.4 bln euros a month. In the first eight months of 2024, the monthly average was 15.4 bln euros. In August, exports were off 6.7% year-over-year and imports were down 4.9% year-over-year.

CNY: The dollar fell to an eight-day low yesterday against the offshore yuan slightly below CNH7.1110, and extended the decline to CNH7.0940 today. This is a new low for the month. The October low was recorded on October 29 near CNH7.0885, and the year's low from September 17 was a little lower, CNH7.0850. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.0865, after fixing it yesterday at CNY7.0833, the lowest since last October. October lending figures were reported showing less of an increase than expected, and money supply growth was slower. China's data deluge is set for tomorrow. October retail sales, industrial output, and fixed asset investment look weaker sequentially. Still, the currency seems less influenced by the macro data and more sensitive to the dollar's broad direction. Meanwhile, the US 10-year premium over China reached the least of the year in late October near 212 bp, which is about 100 bp less than seen in January. The premium is now near 225 bp. China issued a total of $4 bln three- and five-year dollar bonds in Hong Kong last week at nearly the same rate as US yields, and were oversubscribed 30x. Beijing has announced intentions to issue euro-denominated bonds (~4 bln) in Luxembourg next week.

JPY: The dollar traded above JPY155 in North America yesterday for the first time since early February. The gains came despite the pullback in US rates and warnings from Japan's finance ministry. Still, the dollar pulled back to previous resistance near JPY154.50 near midday in NY and edged lower to nearly JPY154.30 today. The policy bind is clear. Due to the prime minister's previous pushback against tighter BOJ policy, the central bank felt compelled to protect its independence, and the government seemed to accept this. Yet, the swaps market has lowered the chances of a hike next month to about 40% from 50% a week ago. A rate hike could also make material intervention in the foreign exchange market more likely. Tomorrow, Japan is expected to report a 0.3% rise in October PPI, the same as in September. The year-over-year pace would slow to 2.5% from 2.7%. The increase in producer prices peaked at 4.3% in February and March. That was the highest since mid-2023. Japan also reports the September tertiary industry index tomorrow. It is likely to have improved after falling by 0.4% in August. Early Monday, Japan reports its first estimate of Q3 GDP. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey envisions a 2.4% annualized contraction. The world's third-largest economy grew by 2.2% in Q2. The weakness of the economy may help explain why the BOJ did not feel comfortable raising rates, but economic traction appeared as the quarter wound down.

GBP: Sterling was sold to $1.3085 in the North American morning before the nearly GBP1.9 bln options expired at $1.3100 yesterday. The (61.8%) of the recent bounce (from almost $1.3000 last week to $1.3190 on Monday) is near $1.3080. Sterling recovered to almost $1.3140 by midday in NY yesterday, and despite disappointing GDP data today, sterling has risen to $1.3170. The UK economy eked out 0.1% growth in Q3, after a 0.3% expansion in Q2 and 0.7% in Q1. The economy contracted 0.1% in September, and August’s 0.1% growth was revised to flat. The sum of the monthly GDP reports in Q3 was -0.2%, after 0.2% in Q2 and 0.6% in Q1. In the quarter, consumption remained subdued (0.2% vs. 0.1%), while government spending slowed (0.3% vs. 1.3%) and total business investment continued to contract (-0.3% vs. -1.1%). In September, industrial output fell 2% and services output crept up (0.2%), construction output rose 0.2% (0.3% in August), and the trade deficit narrowed slightly.

CAD: The US dollar, which peaked last week near CAD1.4140, a seven-month high, fell below CAD1.40 yesterday for the first time this month. It slipped slightly below CAD1.3995 yesterday and eased to CAD1.3985 today, the (61.8%) retracement of the leg up that began after the Federal Reserve and the Bank of Canada cut rates in late October, which is also around the (38.2%) retracement of the larger rally that began with the Fed's September 17 rate cut. Options for almost $500 mln at CAD1.3980 expire today. A break of that area could signal a move toward CAD1.3935 next. It is around where the 200-day moving average and the (50%) retracement of the rally since mid-September are found. Today's manufacturing and wholesales sales figures do not typically have much market impact. However, the Canadian dollar typically underperforms in a softer US dollar context.

AUD: The Australian dollar reached a seven-day high yesterday, surpassed the (50%) retracement of the decline since last month's Federal Reserve rate cut and reached $0.6550. Better employment data and the broadly weaker greenback have seen the Aussie extend its gains to $0.6580 today, surpassing the next retracement (61.8%) and past congestion in the $0.6555-60 area. The high for late October was near $0.6620, and that seems to be a reasonable near-term target. Australia's labor market improved in October, and it reinforces the sense that the central bank will remain on the sidelines for at least the next several months. Australia gained 42.2k jobs overall, which is twice what was expected, and full-time jobs surged by 55.3k. The unemployment rate eased to 4.3% from 4.5%. The futures market does not have much more than a 25% chance of a cut by the end of Q1 '26, and many think that the easing cycle could very well be over.

MXN: The US dollar recorded a two-month high last week near MXN18.77. It has fallen for six consecutive sessions through yesterday, for a cumulative decline of about 2.6%. The greenback was sold to MXN18.2760. The losses were extended to about MXN18.2540 today. It is the lowest level since last month's low, recorded on October 1 around MXN18.24. The low for the year was seen on September 17 (Fed Day) near MXN18.20. Meanwhile, President Sheinbaum's efforts to prepare for next year's USMCA review were dealt a setback. In early September, the government proposed raising the tariff on some 1400 categories of imports from China. However, there was opposition from business, and some within her Morena Party balked, concerned about the dependence on China for machine components, semifinished goods, and raw materials. The current legislative session ends in mid-December.

data.title: Voya Strategic Income Opportunities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Unconstrained and flexible approach, investing broadly across the global debt markets.

Portfolio review

The third quarter of 2025 marked a pivotal shift in the economic narrative, as the labor market long a pillar of resilience began to show signs of strain. Early in the quarter, the June non-farm payrolls (NFP) report surprised to the upside, showing 147,000 jobs added and unemployment dipping to 4.1%. However, this apparent strength proved short-lived. By quarter end, payroll data had been revised sharply lower, revealing a trend closer to 30,000 jobs well below breakeven and an outright decline in June. These developments prompted the U.S. Federal Reserve to resume rate cuts at its September meeting, with the updated dot plot signaling two additional cuts by year end and a more dovish outlook into 2026.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell's remarks at Jackson Hole underscored the fragility of the current labor market equilibrium. He noted that slowing demand was being offset by a decline in labor force participation and immigration, creating a delicate balance that could unravel quickly if layoffs accelerate. This dynamic has helped contain wage pressures, but it also introduces new risks to growth. Meanwhile, inflation remained stubbornly above the Fed's 2% target, with core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index (PCE) rising to 2.9% by quarter end. Notably, core goods inflation accelerated, suggesting that tariff-related pass-through effects are beginning to materialize.

Trade policy developments added further complexity. On July 13, 2025, President Trump announced a 30% tariff on goods from the European Union and Mexico, effective August 1, 2025. Market reaction was muted, likely due to the limited scope of the tariffs and the absence of immediate retaliation. Subsequent trade deals with Japan and the EU helped ease tensions, lowering tariffs from "Liberation Day" levels to 15% and including commitments for foreign investment and increased U.S. imports.

Financial markets responded constructively to the evolving macro backdrop. Rates rallied across the curve, led by the front end, while credit spreads continued to tighten, reflecting investor confidence in the Fed's pivot and the relative stability of corporate and consumer fundamental factors. As a result, most fixed income sectors delivered positive total and excess returns.

For the quarter, the Fund outperformed the Index on a NAV basis. Despite a tick higher in inflation, rates rallied in response to weakening observed in labor data as well as the dovish response from the Fed. The rally was led by the front end, while longer-term rates experienced more muted declines. Due to the Fund's relative long duration position, this meant it contributed to performance. Given the risk-on sentiment over the period, our overweight to spread sectors contributed to relative performance. The largest contributions came from agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), which delivered strong outperformance on the back of falling rate volatility and government-sponsored enterprise (GSE) speculation, and high yield corporates, which continued to tighten in this risk-on environment. Security selection results detracted from relative performance over the quarter, driven by our bias towards shorter-dated investment grade (IG) corporates and our allocation to collateralized mortgage obligations (CMO) given the outperformance of pool securities.

Current strategy and outlook

As we enter the final quarter of 2025, the economic landscape remains shaped by a complex interplay of policy shifts, labor market dynamics, and inflation pressures. Our base case anticipates U.S. growth to remain positive but below potential - likely hovering slightly above 1% as consumers face the headwinds from newly implemented tariffs. However, we think companies may absorb some of the tariff cost, thanks to tailwinds from deregulation and fiscal stimulus via the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), which delivers $300 billion in tax relief. While growth is expected to slow, the economy remains supported by easy financial conditions and targeted fiscal spending abroad, particularly on infrastructure and defense.

Inflation remains a focal point, with mixed signals emerging. Tariff passthrough to consumer prices has been more muted than anticipated, and data shows disinflationary trends in services. This is supportive of our view that tariffs' function more like taxes dampening demand rather than fueling price increases. However, risks persist. The potential for renewed wage pressures, driven by declining immigration and labor force participation, and delayed tariff effects could keep inflation anchored above 3%. The Fed, acknowledging the fragility of the labor market and limited inflation passthrough thus far, has resumed rate cuts and signaled a gradual, data dependent easing path. With fed funds still above neutral, policymakers retain flexibility to respond if conditions deteriorate more quickly than expected.

For fixed income markets, we maintain a cautiously constructive outlook. Yields remain elevated, despite a weakening labor market and a dovish Fed, which allows fixed income to deliver positive returns under a range of potential scenarios. Corporate fundamental factors remain strong, so while further spread tightening appears unlikely, outperformance can still be realized through higher carry.
